← the Atlas

Remote kaval endpoints — kolu dials (P3), the canvas (P4)

feature · seedling ·accepted ·JUN 15, 2026

The forward half of the kaval-sessions plan — kolu-server dialing a remote kaval over ssh (P3, in three phases) and the multiplex canvas it unlocks (P4). Split out of kaval-sessions as the implementation-ready detail outgrew the direction note.

Contents

  • The remote process model (P3)
  • Implementing P3 — three phases
    • The three remote seams
    • Where P3 stops and P4 begins
    • P3 pick-up anchors
    • Open calls — decide at implementation
  • P4 — the canvas: switch or multiplex

The forward half of kaval-sessions. That note carries the R-2 direction and the shipped spike (P0–P2.8, where kaval-tui --host already dials + provisions a remote kaval over ssh); this one is the implementation-ready plan for the two phases still ahead — P3 (kolu-server itself dials a remote kaval, so a canvas tile can live on another host) and P4 (the multiplex canvas on top). The substrate it builds on — one backend, three surfaces (PTY · fs · git) bound to an endpoint, reached by a unix-socket driver (local) or an ssh-stdio HostSession (remote) — is established in the parent; here it gets built.

The remote process model (P3)

kaval and kolu-watcher are two separate processes — forced, not incidental. kaval is the durable survivor (it must outlive deploys); kolu-watcher (provider DAG + fs/git) re-runs fresh (it must be the current build’s code — the #1031 line); one process cannot be both. Locally that role isn’t even a separate process — the DAG + fs/git watchers run inside kolu-server (local.ts: “the DAG runs HERE, in kolu-server”), with kaval the separate daemon. Remotely, kolu-server isn’t on the host, so it splits out as its own kolu-watcher process beside kaval.

And because kaval serves a unix socket (serveOverUnixSocket), reaching it over the network needs a remote stdio endpoint — and a HostSession is one ssh subprocess per host. So the remote host exposes one ssh endpoint — kolu-watcher: kolu-server dials it over one HostSession stdioLink, and kolu-watcher locally fronts kaval (its unix socket) for PTY attach/control + taps while serving its own fs/git + metadata surface (mirrorRemoteCollection). Concretely that is one composed @kolu/surface router served over serveOverStdio — kaval’s ptyHostSurface contract proxied through (kolu-watcher is a client of kaval’s socket) plus the watcher’s own fs/git/metadata — not the contract-blind frontDaemonOverStdio byte-relay kaval-tui uses (P2/P2.5): that relay eats the whole stdio to reach kaval alone, leaving no room for fs/git on the same link, so kolu-watcher cannot use it. The closure is shipped first via provisionAgent (nix copy --derivation → remote realise → pin a GC root). The diagram shows all three boundaries — host (dashed), process (boxed), package (packages/…):

If the host already has a kaval running, it is adopted — the same generic adoptOrEnsure spine (endpoint.ts), per endpoint, that the local boot uses. A duplicate is impossible by construction: a second launch hits the pid-gate’s atomic link(2) EEXIST, liveness-probes the holder (kill(pid,0)), and exits 0 (“yielding to the live instance”) without binding a socket — the running daemon is never disturbed. Re-provisioning is idempotent (nix realise no-ops if the closure is already present; an older build coexists and the moving GC root migrates to the new one). On dial:

the remote kaval is…kolu does
absentprovision + spawn fresh (adoptOrEnsure → ensure)
live · wire-compatibleadopt — connect, never kill; its PTYs reconcile in (older startedAt)
live · a build behindadopt + the B3.4 “update pending” nudge (an orthogonal axis)
live · contract-skewedrecycle (kill → respawn) — only on a typed DaemonContractSkewError, no retry
unreachable (bad dial)bounded retry, then degraded — never killed (a bad dial can’t cost live PTYs)

One kaval per remote host, adopted. Dialing a host joins its kaval — you reconnect to “the prod terminals” — and never clobbers it. The local per-port key (kaval-<port>/, #1313 ), which isolates two dev servers under always-recycle, isn’t needed across ssh: a remote host runs one shared kaval and dials adopt it (the table above), so two reconnects join the same terminals rather than racing to kill them. (The pid-gate is host-local — pid + filesystem on that host — not a cross-host or cross-container lock.)

Implementing P3 — three phases

P3 was over-decomposed. Here it is three phases, shaped by two principles:

  • Front-load every package move. All the structural churn lands in P3.1, while everything is still local and byte-identical — the re-introduced resolver, location on records, creating packages/kolu-watcher and relocating the provider DAG + fs/git surfaces into a module both sides import, the composed watcher contract, and the nix plumbing. Get the renames/moves/copies out of the way first, so P3.2 and P3.3 only add behavior, never shuffle files.
  • Prototype the risky transport outside kolu. The one real unknown — can a single serveOverStdio link carry a proxied daemon contract (kaval’s PTY) and a native surface (fs/git) at once? — needs no kolu change to answer. Prove it in the remote-process-monitor example or drishti first, then adopt the proven shape into kolu-watcher in P3.2. So there is no throwaway-spike phase inside kolu.

The seam is already cut: TerminalEndpoint’s doc-comment names “Remote (P3)” as “the same shape, a different transport,” and P0 ( #1364 ) left three call sites commented // P3 will select the endpoint… — P3.1 is the literal inverse of that commit. P3.1 is a pure refactor/additive (local stays byte-identical); P3.2 and P3.3 each deliver a user-facing increment.

PhaseKindShips — one PR eachGate
P3.1 · structural groundwork — all the package surgery, up frontrefactorGet every move/rename/extraction done while everything is still local and byte-identical: (1) re-introduce TerminalLocation = {kind:'local'} | {kind:'remote',host} + a getTerminalEndpointFor(location) resolver (a Map<hostId, endpoint> seeded local → localTerminalEndpoint, both arms → local) and route the three P0-commented call sites; (2) hostId/location additive on the wire + the server-persisted record; (3) create packages/kolu-watcher and relocate the provider DAG + fs/git surfaces out of packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/ into a lean module both kolu-server (in-process, local) and kolu-watcher (remote) import — kolu-server keeps running the DAG in-process from the moved module, so local is byte-identical; (4) define the composed watcher contract (packages/common/src/watcher-surface.ts: kaval’s ptyHostSurface proxied + the native fs/git/metadata); (5) the KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON nix plumbing (a koluAgentDrvsJson mirror of KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON).full suite green byte-for-byte (the relocated DAG runs identically in-process); resolver unit (local arm === the singleton, remote arm throws, sub-terminal inherits parent location); schema round-trip (pre-P3 blobs default to local); nix build of the new kolu-watcher wrapper
P3.2 · the remote endpoint, end to enduser-facingkolu-watcher runs remotely: it serves the composed router (the PTY half proxied to the host’s kaval — adopt-or-spawned via surface-daemon-supervisor — the fs/git + provider DAG native + host-resident), and RemoteTerminalEndpoint (server/src/terminalEndpoint/remote.ts) binds it via getHostSession({ binary:'kolu-watcher', resolveDrvPath }), one per host. Folds in the remote seams — invert composeSpawnInput (remote $SHELL/$HOME win) + the paste/upload PRECONDITION_FAILED guard — and the cold-realise sync-shadow + failed-unwind. A remote tile with a working shell, Code tab, and agent/git pills. (Adopts the externally-proven transport shape — wire-the-pieces, not discovery.)on a leased pu box: a remote tile types + runs a command; Code tab lists files + shows a real diff; the git pill resolves and claude lights the agent pill — all derived host-resident; $SHELL/$HOME are the remote host’s, no local /nix/store leakage; a bad host → failed, shadow unwound; local tiles byte-identical
P3.3 · remote adoption + resilienceuser-facingAdoption + boot reattach through the unchanged adoptOrEnsure/reconcile spine — a remote connect soul that runs the contract-version handshake and reads the pid-gate over the wire (the RPC-baked system.gateStatus on the watcher contract); per-host DaemonStatus fed from session.onState; degrade-not-recycle for a remote list failure. Remote terminals survive a kolu-server restart (adopt, never clobber).the five-state adoption table on pu (absent / wire-compatible / build-behind / contract-skewed / unreachable→degraded-never-killed); a server restart re-dials + reattaches surviving remote terminals with scrollback; daemonStatus shows the host’s transitions

If P3.2 proves too big in practice, the only clean internal split is the PTY tile first (proxied kaval PTY + the seams) then fs/git + pills — but try it whole; the package work in P3.1 and the external transport prototype are what make one PR feasible.

The three remote seams

The “carries the remote seams” line, resolved concretely — each is small, and each is already de-risked by an existing pattern:

  • composeSpawnInput inversion (P3.2). Today the rich spawn policy (composeSpawnInput, wrapped by buildTerminalSpawnInput in server/src/ptyHost/index.ts) layers env with the local kolu-server process winning over the daemon’s system.info — correct on one machine, wrong over ssh (it would leak the local $HOME/$PATH//nix/store). The remote sibling inverts the precedence: the remote daemon’s system.info wins and only a presentation whitelist (TERM/LANG/LC_*/COLORTERM) rides through. This is exactly buildRemoteCreateInput + REMOTE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH from kaval-tui (P1/P2) — P3 lifts the pattern into kolu-server and routes by endpoint kind. The wire stays self-contained: no var refs, host-relative rc paths.
  • paste/upload PRECONDITION_FAILED guard (P3.2). pasteImage/uploadFile (router.ts) save into a local scratch dir and bracketed-paste that local path into the PTY — unreachable from a remote shell (a silent ENOENT the agent would chase). P3’s scope is the guard, not the fix: inspect the tile’s recorded location (on the record after P3.2) and throw PRECONDITION_FAILED on a remote tile. The actual copy-to-remote-scratch is P4.
  • drvPath via resolveSystem (P3.1 nix plumbing + the contract; P3.2 dials). kolu-server must ship the right-arch kolu-watcher closure: resolveSystem(host) (the P2.8-memoized ssh arch probe) → a {system → .drv} map baked onto the server wrapper as KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON (the exact mirror of kaval’s KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON), fed to getHostSession. The pool key is (host, binary) — one resolver per host.

Where P3 stops and P4 begins

P3 makes a remote terminal correct and observable on the server; P4 makes it discoverable, controllable, and fully featured in the UI. So P3 ends with hostId on the wire that the client reads but renders nothing host-aware — tiles, dock and chrome are visually byte-identical for local and remote. P4 adds the active-kaval strip, the per-tile host hint, the ssh-config picker, the reconnect affordance (a button wired to session.reconnect()), and the real paste/upload-to-remote that turns P3.2’s guard into a feature. The sharp line: anything that renders a host indicator, a picker, or a recovery control — or transfers a file to the remote host — is P4.

P3 pick-up anchors

Concrete hooks, every path verified against the tree (the P1/P2 anchor tables in the parent set the convention):

ConcernWhere it lands
dispatch seamTerminalLocation back in packages/common/src/terminalEndpoint.ts (its doc already names the remote binding); new leaf packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/resolve.ts (the inverse of the deleted terminalBackend/index.ts, commit 1c8f1770); re-route terminals.ts (the // P3 will select… comment), surface.ts (the fs/git stream block), router.ts (terminal.attach)
wire / recordpackages/common/src/surface.ts — hostId on TerminalInfoSchema, location on ServerPersistedTerminalFieldsSchema (.optional()); TerminalCreateInputSchema in contract.ts. Heed the metadata-partition comment — misclassifying a new field onto the wrong base is the one silent failure mode
remote transportimport getHostSession · HostSession · resolveSystem · mirrorRemoteCollection · makeClientCursor · isLocalHost · FailureCause from packages/surface-nix-host/src/index.ts; new packages/server/src/terminalEndpoint/remote.ts; new packages/common/src/watcher-surface.ts; new packages/kolu-watcher — the host-resident process. It serves a composed router via serveOverStdio (@kolu/surface): the PTY half a client of kaval’s unix socket (kaval adopt-or-spawned via @kolu/surface-daemon-supervisor’s adoptOrEnsure), the fs/git/metadata half native (kolu-git on the remote disk + the unchanged DAG from terminalEndpoint/providers.ts). Not frontDaemonOverStdio — that’s P2’s kaval-only raw relay
the three seamscomposeSpawnInput/buildTerminalSpawnInput in packages/server/src/ptyHost/index.ts; inversion pattern at packages/kaval-tui/src/create.ts (buildRemoteCreateInput + REMOTE_ENV_PASSTHROUGH); paste guard at router.ts (pasteImage/uploadFile/bracketedPastePath); resolveSystem in surface-nix-host/src/arch.ts
adoption spine (reused unchanged)adoptOrEnsure in packages/surface-daemon-supervisor/src/endpoint.ts; pure partition in packages/server/src/reconcile.ts; adoptSurvivingSession in terminalEndpoint/reattach.ts (parallel the remote one); the local connect soul to mirror in ptyHost/connect.ts; DaemonStatus already keyed by hostId in ptyHost/daemonStatus.ts
nixflake.nix (kavalAgentDrvsJson → add a koluAgentDrvsJson sibling) + default.nix (the KAVAL_AGENT_DRVS_JSON --set-default line → add KOLU_AGENT_DRVS_JSON, plus openssh/nix on the server wrapper PATH)

Open calls — decide at implementation

Genuine design calls left to the implementer, each with a recommendation:

  1. Where fs/git lives — kolu-watcher runs kolu-git natively + the DAG in-process; kaval stays a pure PTY host (co-locates agent detection with the JSONL files it reads). Recommended.
  2. Remote gate read — an RPC-baked system.gateStatus() on the watcher contract, not an ssh-exec subprocess; bake it into the P3.1 contract so P3.3 needs no wire bump. Recommended.
  3. Pooling — one HostSession per remote host, keyed (host, 'kolu-watcher'), shared across the PTY + fs/git + metadata pumps. Recommended.
  4. Create-time selection — explicit location > parent inheritance > default-local; the interactive picker is P4. Recommended.
  5. Remote list failure — degrade, don’t recycle (a pure-dial remote may not be kolu’s to own — unlike the local F3 “recycle on list failure”, which assumes kolu owns the lifecycle). Recommended; settle in P3.3.

P4 — the canvas: switch or multiplex

With P3 the server can hold N live endpoints; P4 spends that on the UI — and settles the one open granularity call. The same substrate answers either, so it is a later, reversible choice that the P1–P2 spike deliberately did not depend on:

switch (tmux-style)multiplex (canvas) — leaning
the canvasone kaval in viewtiles span kavals
per-tile locationunneededadditive — the R-1 discriminator, finally used
live endpointsone, swappedN concurrent
host hintone current pointera light per-tile tag; ChromeBar shows the active kavals
why it fits kolusimplestthe canvas is spatial + heterogeneous by design

The lean is multiplex: tmux-switch is a terminal-multiplexer metaphor, but kolu is a canvas — “watch a local build beside a prod-ssh tile” is the point of a canvas, not a corner case. The cost over switch is modest and clean — per-tile location returns, but additively (no RemoteTerminalBackend, no screen mirror — just which endpoint holds this tile’s PTY), and the ChromeBar shows only the kavals this canvas actually has tiles on.

P4 builds multiplex, then validates the feel. The UI work consumes exactly the server substrate P3 left: the active-kaval strip (local always present), the per-tile host hint, the ssh-config picker (how a user chooses a remote host — P3’s selection is config/env-driven, not interactive), the reconnect affordance wired to session.reconnect() and tooltip’d from lastError, and turning P3.2’s paste/upload PRECONDITION_FAILED guard into a real feature (copy the file to the remote scratch dir, bracketed-paste the remote path). Then the validation: does N-at-once actually beat tmux-switch in daily use? Fall back to switch if multiplex doesn’t earn its keep — the granularity call lands and is validated here, on real use, not up front.

← the Atlas github ↗
